author: @pendethan
text:
It is a friction drive. The friction pairing in this transmission is the contact surface between the cone and the swash plate.

Generally, every transmission has three torque inputs, mostly via two rotating shafts and the non-rotating casing. The sum of all impacting torques is always zero. The transmission acts as a torque splitter, independent of which input is rotated or stationary. At all times, the highest torque equals the sum of the other torques impacting in reversed direction. As this is a positive reduction transmission with a ratio somewhere between 2 (swash plate at 60° angle) and ∞ (0° angle), the sum of the torque at the input shaft and the torque at the cage casing equals the torque at the output shaft. At ratio 2, the torque at the casing is equal to the torque at the input shaft, and we have twice the torque at the output shaft. The torque at the cage casing must be a result of a force transmitted from somewhere within the mechanism. This is the friction force acting at the contact surface between the cone, fixed to the casing cage, and the swash plate, fixed to the output shaft.

Another way of looking at it is when we swap rotating inputs: By making the input shaft stationary with the roller mechanism now fixed in place, but instead rotating the casing cage with the cone, nothing about the transmission and the acting torques will change. However, it now becomes much easier to see that the rotating cone is in fact driving the swash plate via a friction pairing at the contact surface.

author: @tomp7141
text:
Forcing a disk to nutate, by using a roller or whatever, will not cause the disk to rotate about its axis unless the edge of that disk is in a no-slip engagement with a surface.  That is, friction contact.
In other words, if there was no friction between the nutating disk and the cone-shaped base, then no torque could be transmitted through this device.

author: @willyleavitt-pe1cw
text: It’s a mechanic version of the hydrostatic transmission used in Honda ATV’s. That came out in the early 2000’s . Interesting story. It’s a system used in farm equipment for many decades but the efficiency was only about 15% if I remember correctly. In the late 60’s Mr Honda himself redesigned it to bring the efficiency up to 96%. They didn’t have a product at the time that was suitable so it got tested , proven and the put on a shelf until the time it was needed. That time came with the popularity of mid size off road ATV’s in the late 90’s and so off the shelf it came and was put to use. The best part is the control system of a small electric stepper motor used to change the plate angle through a shaft with an extremely low friction bearing system.

replies.text: @willyleavitt-pe1cw hydrostatic transmissions traditionally had about 80% efficiency

author: @JamesCopeland-i1j
text:
"Doesn't use friction"?

Missed me on that one. Swashplate transmissions have been used in some niche applications, where the ratio sweep/range is fairly narrow, and load is light. They're not particularly useful anywhere else. Even when the plate is built considerably robust... they develop distortions and wear patterns in short order, which render them virtually useless.

replies.author: @user-hachemimebarek
replies.text: If you pay attention a little. It's not friction-based. Look at the contact point between the rocker disc and the drum. It's an angled contact, offset from the direction of rotation. Transmission depends on the inclined surface, not friction. It's like a nut and bolt. The inclined grooves are what lock it. Of course, friction will be generated, but friction alone isn't enough to prevent the nut from rotating freely around the bolt. If we didn't have inclined grooves, sorry for the bad English. It's machine translation.

author: @Berkeloid0
text: A couple of comments have mentioned the "Way Out West - Workshop Stuff" YouTube channel already, but his use of the friction CVT you describe at the start of the video has an interesting property that seems uncommon with the other solutions, which is that you can run the output in reverse as well (by moving the wheel past the centre of the flat surface).  Many CVT designs only seem to operate in one direction, which complicates the design if you also want to run the output in reverse sometimes.

author: @Pystro
text:
CVTs are a good _mechanical_ solution to the problem of varying wind power. The problem is that as far as I know, nobody has come up with a CVT that has as little loss as a normal transmission - and that might even be fundamentally impossible.
I wonder if there could be an electric solution. Give the generator 2 sets of windings and you have 4 ways of switching them; in series, in parallel and only each single winding. Switch them with relays instead of power semiconductors, and you have basically zero loss (except for contact surface resistance). And with electronic or relay based switching, you could switch back and forth between gears quickly in order to keep the blade speed at a desired value (where "quickly" might even mean as slow as once per revolution of the blades). Which technically gives you infinite gears (except that the in-between gears won't be as efficient as the "true electronic gears". And if you need more gears than 4, you can just add a classical mechanical gearbox or use more than 2 sets of windings.
Don't take that as me discouraging you, though. If someone would come up with a CVT that performs as well as a classic transmission that would be huge.

author: @Deviation4360
text: That is actually a clever frictionless clutch plate if anything. I picture the slight angular advance/retard (created by the angle of the swashplate translating into relative position change of the roller arm) between the drive and driven shafts (with its gear as you showed) being used to adjust the blade pitch of a wind turbine as the driven/or generator shaft swash plate rises to create the advance while simultaneously engaging the whole mechanism as a clutch. Extra air resistance type blades would be needed to push the clutch pack and whole propeller assembly backwards as a force to engage/disengage the clutch though. Then it's certainly usable as you intially proposed.

author: @Berkana
text: Robert, around 5:21 you showed that the plate is connected to a shaft using a universal joint, but universal joints do not transmit rotation evenly; the output of a universal joint is inevitably lumpy. To get smooth transmission, you need two universal joints in a row where the shaft angles going through the chain of joints are evenly distributed. Or you can use a constant speed coupling such as a Thompson coupling, which essentially is two universal joints all smashed into one plane, with some linkages to enforce even distribution of angles.

author: @dudleysmith5111
text: When you operate a standard universal joint at an angle (such as in this swashplate design), the input and output speeds are different. Only with a constant velocity joint will the input and output speeds be the same for an angled drive. How might that difference affect the output speed desired. Great discussion and modeling. Thanks for the resources and making us think!

author: @SyntacticOperator
text: I don't quite understand why generators have a limit to how fast they can spin, and generate electricity. Doesn't the electricity leave the coils at the speed of light? I don't understand why the electricity can't be shuttled out as fast as it's generated? Doesn't it depend on the size of the outgoing conductors? Or does it depend on the size of the coils themselves? Is it because the voltage is too low? The way in which they are wound?

author: @easyvolts2576
text: I don't think it's a CVT, it can work as a clutch, but i'm not sure if it can change RPM of the output (because angular speed on the input and output seems to be the same). Could you please demonstrate  how this device changes speed of the rotation?

replies.author: @ChrisZost
replies.text: @easyvolts2576 It is a hydraulic pump/motor combination with a swash plate to regulate pump displacement. Have one on my garden tractor.

replies.author: @Kargoneth
replies.text: Indeed. The rate at which the input and output rotate is the same.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i don't mind you don't think it is a CVT mate - it's up to you what you think - but you could research it if you wanted

replies.author: @abdabzeebop2
replies.text: Its a ct, not a cvt, ie it doesn't vary input vs output speed; it just varies the distance from the axis where 2 surfaces touch; the relationship between that distance on the input end vs the ouput end doesn't change, so no variable speed.

author: @tates11
text: The swash plate pump is nice and simple as the flow can drive a hydraulic motor. A variable offset vane pump can do the same, and with a feedback loop, it can alter the offset on the fly as the load changes.
